title: Chicken Xacuti

overview.description: A complex Goan chicken curry made with roasted whole spices, coconut and aromatics, creating a deeply flavoured, fragrant and gently warming sauce.

ingredients: 2 tbsp neutral oil or coconut oil
1 tbsp tamarind concentrate, or to taste
method: Season the chicken with the salt.
Toast the grated coconut in a dry frying pan until lightly golden. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, toast the coriander seeds, cumin seeds, fennel seeds, peppercorns, dried chillies, cloves, cinnamon, cardamom, star anise and the poppy and sesame seeds if using.
Toast over medium heat until fragrant, taking care not to burn the spices.
Allow the toasted ingredients to cool.
Blend the toasted spices and coconut with the garlic, ginger, turmeric and enough water to make a smooth paste.
Heat the oil in a large heavy-based pan.
Brown the chicken pieces in batches if necessary, then remove and set aside.
Add the sliced onions to the pan.
Cook until softened and deeply golden.
Add the spice paste and cook for 3–5 minutes until fragrant.
Add the chopped tomatoes and cook until softened.
Return the chicken to the pan.
Add the coconut milk and water.
Cover and cook until the chicken is tender and cooked through.
Stir in the tamarind and adjust the seasoning.
Simmer uncovered for a few minutes if the sauce needs thickening.
Finish with fresh coriander and serve.

learning.did-you-know: Xacuti is one of Goa's best-known spice-rich curries. The name is often written as xacuti or shakuti, reflecting the region's Portuguese influence and varied transliteration.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How dry-toasting whole spices and coconut develops depth and how a complex spice paste creates a layered curry without relying on a large number of fresh ingredients.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A complex regional curry that demonstrates how roasting spices and coconut can create depth and complexity through technique rather than simply adding more chilli.
chapman-tips: Toast the spices gently; burnt spices will make the curry bitter.
Toast the coconut separately because it burns more quickly than the whole spices.
Don't rush the onions. Deeply softened onions add important sweetness and depth.
The spice paste should be as smooth as your blender can make it.
Bone-in chicken thighs give the richest flavour, but boneless thighs are easier for family meals.
The tamarind should provide a gentle sour note rather than dominate the curry.
If the curry is too spicy, add more coconut milk rather than simply adding water.
notes.batch-cooking: Chicken xacuti freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently until piping hot.
notes.leftovers: Leftover xacuti is excellent with rice, roti or bread. The sauce can also be used as a filling for a wrap.
